insights.insight: Ad tech charges $5–$150 CPA for probabilistic attribution; this protocol settles at roughly $0.10 per cryptographically verified receipt — a 50–200x cost delta that isn't an efficiency gain, it's a structural replacement of the attribution problem itself.

With Chrome completing cookie deprecation and device fingerprinting increasingly blocked by regulation and browser privacy updates, the industry has no cross-site deterministic signal at scale — verified receipt tokens are the only identity-free alternative that doesn't depend on a deprecated primitive.

Amazon sees Amazon, Walmart sees Walmart — no major retailer has visibility into purchases made outside its own walls; at 3,893 verified receipts spanning Walmart, Kroger, Dollar General, and Home Depot, this dataset is the first cross-ecosystem purchase signal that doesn't require a data-sharing agreement or identity disclosure.

Nine non-metro state designations are active across 17 CBSAs, with adoption following social graphs rather than metro density — a word-of-mouth spread pattern that reaches the physical-retail consumer segment most underrepresented in digital-first targeting stacks.

Existing receipt platforms like Fetch and Ibotta hold real purchase data but it is identity-attached, siloed, and not queryable by third parties — brands accessing those networks carry regulatory exposure that identity-free verified tokens structurally eliminate.

The verified GMV figure — $227,031 across 3,893 receipts — is less important as a current number than as a compounding mechanism: query value rises with dataset size, which increases reward pools, which attracts more receipts; brands that delay access will face a higher entry cost as the dataset matures.
